summary: The author placed 19th by constructing a generative pipeline that synthesizes images via diffusion models and converts them into optimized SVG format. Key technical decisions included compressing SVG outputs by replacing absolute coordinates with relative paths and grouping same-colored elements, alongside evaluating generation quality using two independent VQA questions focused on shape and color fidelity. The inference loop was tightly controlled by a 63-second time budget per prediction, utilizing batch generation of three candidates and early termination to dynamically carry over unused compute time.
extensive_summary:
## Core Generative Pipeline
The solution centers on a diffusion-based image generation workflow followed by a vectorization step. The author explicitly experimented with multiple diffusion architectures, naming **PixART** and **Sana** as the primary models tested during development.

## SVG Compression & Optimization
A major technical focus was the bitmap-to-SVG conversion algorithm. Rather than relying on standard raster-to-vector tools, the author engineered a compression strategy that replaces absolute coordinate paths with relative paths and merges adjacent geometric elements sharing identical color values. This optimization directly improved the final scoring metric.

## VQA-Driven Evaluation & Inference Control
Instead of traditional pixel-wise or perceptual metrics, the pipeline employs two independent Visual Question Answering (VQA) prompts to assess shape and color fidelity. Inference is tightly constrained by a fixed 63-second time budget per prediction. The system generates three candidate images per batch, evaluates them against the VQA prompts, and implements early termination once a predefined threshold score is met. Unused time is dynamically carried over to subsequent predictions, creating a resource-aware generation loop that maximizes throughput within strict latency constraints.
competition_flow: The pipeline generates images using a diffusion model (PixART or Sana), converts the output bitmaps to optimized SVGs using relative paths and color grouping, evaluates them via two VQA questions focused on shape and color, and manages inference time with a 63-second budget and early termination.
insights: Optimizing SVG compression via relative paths and color grouping was the most impactful factor for the final score.
Using two independent VQA questions focused on shape and color provided an effective evaluation metric for generated images.
Implementing a strict time budget with early termination and time carry-over significantly improved inference efficiency.
